sentences.text: When I speak--
How much-- how much of my body are you showing?
You would rather I didn't fold them?
My name is Henry Aizenman.
H-E-N-R-Y A-I-Z-E-N-M-A-N.
At birth, it was-- my first name was Chaim Tzvi, which is Hebrew.
No.
Aizenman was spelled A-J-Z-E-N-M-A-N.
No.
Yeah, I was called [?
Henyek. ?] And sometimes I was called Fat Guy.
I was born on March 30, 1931-- March 20, excuse me.
I was born in Warsaw, but--
--I lived most of my life in Przytyk.
Przytyk was a town of about roughly 2,000 people, most of them Jewish.
It was a sort of a shtetl.
There must have been several hundred gentiles.
But on Mondays, there was usually a market day.
And the-- the place used to be packed with farmers from surrounding areas.
Oh, yeah.
There-- there was one large synagogue and several small stiebels.
The-- the relationship was pretty miserable.

As a matter of fact, one day, when I was six years old, in 19-- when I was five years old, in 1936, on a Monday, the people, particularly the peasants from the surrounding areas, started beating up on the Jews and wanted to kill all the Jews for one reason or another.
Well, the Jews defended themselves.
And subsequently, several of the Jews who defended themselves wound up in prison.
It was against the law to defend yourself.
Not that I know of.
Please remember that I'm talking as a child, and--
--my memories are from the viewpoint of a child.
They were the victims.
It was sort of keep a low profile [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and maybe you won't get hurt.
Well--
--the people, they were mostly artisans.
And they had their stores.
My-- my parents, for instance-- my father had a-- a shop that sold and repaired sewing machines and bicycles.
And he used to sell many, many sewing machines and bicycles, also repair them.
My grandparents on my mother's side, they-- my-- my grandfather had a liquor store, which in Poland was a monopoly.
And my grandmother and he both had a dry goods store.
And during the pogrom, which is what that was when they started beating up on us, the-- all the stores-- first of all, the peasants started looting the liquor store.
And my grandfather tried to keep them out, but he was knocked down.
And one of my uncles, one of my father's brothers, was considered a hero because he was defending his parents.
And they wanted to put him in jail, but he had a good lawyer.
My father owned a firearm, because at one time, he had been a traveling salesman selling Singer sewing machines from the back of a horse.
And he had kept his firearm.
But fortunately, he never fired it.
But they still tried to indict him for defending himself.
I remember our store was completely demolished.
And I remember for-- for months afterwards, my father and mother both spent their time cleaning up.
My father's name was Baruch.
As a matter of fact, I named one of my kids after him, except I called him Burton.
Hannah.
My granddaughter's name is Hannah.
No.
My mother was mostly-- it was very seldom that people in Przytyk had their wives working.
It was not a-- a-- a woman's place to work in those days.
My-- my father had gone to a yeshiva when he was young.
But he-- subsequently, he became less religious.
I got most of my religious upbringing from my grandparents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was a Zionist.
And I guess that sort of interfered with his religious life.
Because according to the very religious Jews, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the state of Israel was not supposed to come into being until the Messiah arrived.
And the Messiah hadn't arrived at the time.
But my parents both believed that the Jews ought to have a homeland.
As a matter of fact, when I was six years old, after that pogrom, we tried to emigrate to Palestine at the time.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they wanted to bring most of their savings, their money with them.
At the time, the British made up rules about what you can bring into Palestine and what you can't.
And one of the rules was that there was a limited amount of money you could bring in.
So my parents decided they-- they didn't want to go without any money.
So they were killed with their money.
Yeah, we were-- they were talking about it.
As a matter of fact, one of the pictures that I have left of my father was when he was on Theodor Herzl's grave in Vienna.
When he was a young man, about 19, he went to Vienna and had his picture taken on Herzl's grave.
Oh, yeah.
I mean, most-- mostly everybody attended synagogue.
Like, for instance, my grandfather would attend synagogue, one of the stiebels, just about every day.
But, for instance, my parents would go to the synagogue only on-- on Shabbos, on the Sabbath, and on the-- on the holidays.
I was a-- I was a fairly precocious child.
And I learned not only the Bible, but also the Talmud.
By the time I was five years old, I was studying that.
No.
It was-- it was wonderful.
I was a spoiled brat, and I loved every minute of it.
I was the first kid in town-- the only kid for a long time-- to have a bicycle.
It was a specially built bicycle.
So I could ride it.
And I had all my friends ride it.
Sometimes I charged them a nickel for riding it.
I made quite a bit of money.
I was going to kindergarten-type school-- it was a Hebrew-type school-- when I was four years old.
And then, when I was six or six and a half, I would go to public school, which is the Polish school.
And after school, I would go to Hebrew school just about every day.
And in the wintertime, it would be dark by the time I got home from school.
And then I would do my homework.
But I went-- I was pretty smart in those days, even though I have deteriorated now.
No.
Only at Hebrew school.
At the other school, they were mostly non-Jews.
And of course, for-- we had to leave the room for-- when-- when they said the prayers, which were the Catholic prayers.
Poland was essentially a Catholic country.
Well, they made us leave the classroom.
We-- they didn't want us defile their prayers.
I didn't have too many non-Jewish friends.
I had some, but not very many.
They-- they-- they didn't mix.
We didn't mix.
I assume my parents had some gentile friends, but not very many.
It was a cultural gap.
Yeah, all of them.
It-- I-- I only-- actually, I only went to school a couple of years over there.
But they treated us more on the basis of knowledge and brains rather than-- or accomplishment, rather than according to religion.
Oh, yes.
I had-- I had-- for instance, in the house next to ours, I had two cousins.
One was a year younger than I was, and one was a year older.
And then I had a girlfriend.
I mean, at six years old, can you imagine?
I had a girlfriend.
Her name was Rochelle.
And they lived across the town square.
As a matter of fact, her father was the one who-- he was sentenced to one year in prison for defending himself during the pogrom.
It was-- it was almost-- almost an idyllic life.
We would run, bathe in the summertime.
We would skate on the frozen river in the wintertime.
We would go fishing.
And it was a lot of fun.
I-- I didn't have too much spare time during school year because I attended both regular school and Hebrew school.
I remember we were running around on the river in Poland.
Things didn't thaw out as easily as they do in Birmingham.
And I think what-- it must have been in March or in April, we were running along on the frozen river and I fell in because it started thawing out.
And they dragged me out.
And I was the apple of both my parents', as well as both sets of grandparents', eyes.
And they immediately called a-- a doctor.
I called him a witch doctor, actually, because he wasn't a real doctor.
But he could give you shots.
And every time I started coughing or anything, they put those cups on me.
I don't know if you know what the cups are.
They were called bankes in-- in Yiddish.
And they'd put them on.
They'd put a-- a match inside of them, and then put them on your back.
And they would stick there.
And every time I had to get those things, I hated it.
But I did as I was told.
It was done regularly several times a year to keep me healthy.
But it-- it was-- it was a-- a-- it was a nice life.
I mean, aside from occasional bouts of anti-Semitism and pogroms, things were going well.
It was-- it was-- well, actually, there was-- there was a lawyer.
And there was a doctor and witch doctor.
Occasionally, there was a doctor.
And there was a pharmacy in town.
And I-- I didn't have that much to do with them.
Again, remember, I'm speaking from the standpoint of a child.
Oh, yeah.
I-- I didn't know what-- what it meant.
But I was constantly aware because they were talking about it.
My parents were considered part of the intelligentsia-- you know, well educated.
And for instance, my maternal grandfather was considered one of the just men, like when somebody-- when there was a dispute between two people, they would bring it to my grandfather.
And my parents were one of the first people to have a radio.
Can you imagine that?
We hardly ever had electricity.
And when we didn't have electricity, they would-- they would listen to the radio on batteries.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they read all kinds of newspapers.
That's where I learned how to speak different languages, by reading newspapers in-- in both-- in Polish, in Hebrew, in Yiddish, and also some German.
What sorts of things did you listen to on the radio?
It wasn't believe me.
We listened to the news, mostly, and commentary.
And I remember I used to-- I used to make up stories how does the radio work.
I mean, as a six and seven-year-old, there must be a little man inside the radio who is talking.
Otherwise, how could-- how could you hear anything he says?
No.
We were-- were were doing our own entertaining.
I remember I-- I once saw a movie in Przytyk.
And it was hand cranked.
No.
But when I was about six or-- six, I was taken to Warsaw, as a matter-- to see a doctor for a-- a tooth extraction.
And one of my cousins took me to a movie.
And I saw Errol Flynn in And I was scared spitless, really, because that's the first time I'd ever seen a movie that had sound in it.
Oh, we were-- we were singing, and all kinds of games with reading, pencil and paper.
And with-- with my friends, of course, we were doing all kinds of sports, playing what-- what we call here soccer.
But we called it football over there.
Well, we used to go and watch soccer games where the-- where the older kids were playing.
But we youngsters, we had sort of unorganized soccer games.
I would say they had the-- the equivalent almost of a college education, my father in particular.
He was educated in the yeshiva, as well as a-- a grade school.
And grade school education in Poland was pretty well structured.
And it-- it was almost the same as a-- a high school education over here.
I personally attended two years of school over there.
And it took me until now-- I'm still catching up on my education.
My grandparents mostly had religious education.
There were many, many Jewish people in-- in Przytyk who did not speak much of the Polish language [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is actually amazing to-- when you think about it now.
But my-- both my parents and grandparents spoke Polish like natives, which they were.
Yiddish, mostly.
And also-- they used to get Polish, and they used to get some German stations.
I think it was a crystal set.
I don't know how far those things reach.
I remember I-- I once listened-- I once listened to a Polish minister make a speech.
And he says, we'll never give in to Germany, because Germany wanted a part of Poland before the war.
We will never give in to what the Germans want.
Of course, it didn't take them long to back down.
Yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of the memories that stand out are when [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we would-- when the Germans were attacking Poland, we went to a farm.
Our whole family went to a-- a farm of-- that a-- a gentile friend of ours owned.
And we stayed there for about a week.
By that time, the war was over and the Germans were-- had taken Poland.
And one of my first memories was the Germans were taking collaborators.
They had collaborators, Polish people who helped them.
And my grandfather, who was pretty old as far as I was concerned-- he was probably in his 50's.
He had a-- a beard.
And most religious people wore the religious garb, like a black-- a black frock coat, et cetera.
And I remember they-- they-- they needed some work done.
Either they-- they claimed they wanted some work done.
And they-- they insisted that-- they grabbed him, put him on a-- on a-- on a cart, and so they-- and so he should dig ditches.
And my father, who-- came up there and volunteered to take his place.
But they knocked him away.
And they didn't want him to take his place.
They wanted to humiliate my grandfather.
Yes--
--with some Polish collaborators.
Yes.
It didn't seem that they reacted too severely to the defeat.
Of course, the Germans made them happy by humiliating the Jewish people.
And the Polish people were happy about that.
Really, not much for the first few months, except some annoyances like they grabbed them and made them dig ditches or fix the roads and things like that.
But eventually, they told us we would have to move out of town.
And--
Who knows?
The Germans were in charge.
Evidently.
But I-- I can't go into details--
--'cause I don't know.
About six-- six, seven months.
And then they made us go to a-- to a ghetto in-- in another town.
Called Jedlinsk, spelled J-E-D-L-I-N-S-K.
And over there, we moved into a house.
It was extremely crowded.
There must've been about 12 people in the house.
About five, six.
Well, the living conditions weren't that bad because the-- there were only annoyances.
I'm speaking by comparison with what happened later.
I remember I-- I got really sick.
It was either typhus or typhoid fever.
There was an epidemic going on.
And I recovered pretty easily.
And my grandparents and my parents both took care of me.
And I had one aunt who was within five years or-- she was five years older than I was.
Her name Gucia.
And she took care of me.
And eventually, they made us go to a work camp.
I really don't know.
When I wanted food, I asked my mother--
--or my grandmother.
Only-- only haphazardly.
I was taking-- I was getting Hebrew lessons from my grandfather.
But--
No.
The-- that's-- that's when we started getting cut off--
--from old friends and family, when we were in the ghetto.
I mean, we had a huge family that had lived in Poland for hundreds and hundreds of years.
In the ghetto, there were my grandparents, two of my aunts, my parents, and I, and a few other relatives.
No, it didn't have walls.
They put up some kind of a-- a-- fences.
But it didn't have walls.
It was-- it was very dangerous to venture outside, which some people did.
You were-- you were either put in jail or shot.
It just happened.
You see, I think of it as sort of-- I call it salami tactics, where they abrogate a little bit of your freedom.
They cut off one piece of salami, and then another piece of salami.
By the time you get through, there's nothing left.
And you're practically used to-- to the whole thing.
Only from hearsay.
I didn't-- I-- I didn't personally see any.
I know that we were very crowded.
And my parents were complaining about not being able to get enough food.
But we were fairly wealthy.
In-- in the ghetto, they still helped each other.
It was subsequently where it was every-- every dog for himself.
Yeah, we used to-- we used to, for instance-- we used to come together and drink coffee and schnapps, and occasionally cake.
And we would sing all kinds of songs, most of them anti-German.
We thought we were real revolutionaries.
